subject: ap-psychology
title: Motivation, Personality, and Social Psychology — Concept Application

parts.prompt: Explain how Soundwave amplitude relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that relevant to the scenario, a loud sound is associated with high  soundwaves, or a soft sound is associated with low soundwaves.

required_elements: The height of the soundwaves that were created when the lamp crashed must have been very high because Steve referred to it as loud. The sound of the lamp crashing was so loud because it had a high amplitude. Steve did not hear the timer chime because it has low soundwaves, and he almost burned the eggs.
alternatives.correct_example: The height of the soundwaves that were created when the lamp crashed must have been very high because Steve referred to it as loud. The sound of the lamp crashing was so loud because it had a high amplitude. Steve did not hear the timer chime because it has low soundwaves, and he almost burned the eggs.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must connect amplitude to loudness: a loud sound (like the lamp crashing) has high amplitude/high soundwaves, and a soft sound has low amplitude/low soundwaves. Responses that confuse amplitude with pitch (frequency) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The height of the soundwaves when the lamp crashed must have been very high because Steve referred to it as a loud sound — indicating high amplitude.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly equates loudness with high amplitude and applies it to the lamp crash in the scenario.

sample_responses.response: The sound of the lamp crashing was so loud because it had a high amplitude.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — directly states the loud crash had high amplitude, accurately applying the concept.

sample_responses.response: The crash of the lamp had a high pitch.
sample_responses.commentary: Pitch is determined by frequency, not amplitude. This response confuses amplitude with a different acoustic property.

parts.prompt: Explain how Procedural memory relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Steve and/or the children remember how to physically do  something in the scenario (e.g., making breakfast) without paying conscious attention (e.g.,  automatically, unconsciously, muscle memory, nondeclarative, well-practiced/learned) to  doing so.

required_elements: The children have set the breakfast table so much that they do it automatically.
alternatives.correct_example: The children have set the breakfast table so much that they do it automatically.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Procedural memory requires (1) a physical/motor action and (2) automaticity (done without conscious attention, automatically, via muscle memory). Responses that describe declarative memory (knowing facts or sequences) do not earn the point. Responses that describe remembering a location without it being a motor action do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Steve makes breakfast automatically because he has done it so many times — his procedural memory kicks in so he doesn't have to consciously think about each step.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a practiced physical skill (making breakfast) performed without conscious attention, consistent with procedural memory.

sample_responses.response: The children have set the breakfast table so much that they do it automatically, using procedural memory.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable CB example — setting the table is a well-practiced motor routine performed automatically.

sample_responses.response: Steve remembered that the breakfast was cooking, so he had to go in and keep it from burning.
sample_responses.commentary: This is declarative/semantic memory (knowing a fact), not procedural memory (a motor skill performed automatically).

sample_responses.response: The kids know they are supposed to take out the plates and then open the cupboard to find juice glasses.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes a conscious, step-by-step sequence of declarative knowledge rather than an automatic motor skill.

parts.prompt: Explain how Diffusion of responsibility relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the children do not attempt to clean up the broken lamp  because they assume others are likely to do it.

required_elements: The children are not cleaning up the lamp because they think someone else will do it. The younger children assume the older children will clean up the lamp.
alternatives.correct_example: The children are not cleaning up the lamp because they think someone else will do it. The younger children assume the older children will clean up the lamp.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must explain that each child fails to help because they assume someone else in the group will take responsibility. Responses that attribute inaction to a lack of accountability, distraction by TV, or deliberate choice to wait do not earn the point. The key element is the assumption that someone else will act.

sample_responses.response: The children are not cleaning up the lamp because each one assumes that one of the other siblings will do it — this is diffusion of responsibility.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies that the presence of multiple bystanders leads each child to assume another will act.

sample_responses.response: The younger children assume the older children will clean up the lamp.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — shows the mechanism of diffusion of responsibility (assuming another specific person will intervene).

sample_responses.response: The children don't clean up the lamp because they are not responsible for their actions.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes irresponsibility as a trait, not diffusion of responsibility as a social phenomenon arising from group membership.

sample_responses.response: The children are watching TV, so they don't clean up the lamp.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains inaction as distraction rather than as the result of assuming someone else will act.

parts.prompt: Explain how Dispositional attribution relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Steve and/or the children overestimated the internal factors  influencing their own or someone else's behavior in the scenario.

required_elements: Steve decided that his children were clumsy and irresponsible. Steve concludes that he is a mean person for disciplining his children.
alternatives.correct_example: Steve decided that his children were clumsy and irresponsible. Steve concludes that he is a mean person for disciplining his children.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Dispositional attribution means attributing someone's behavior to internal characteristics (personality, traits, disposition) rather than situational factors. The response must show a character making an internal attribution about themselves or another person in the scenario. Responses that describe situational attribution (external factors, like distraction) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Steve decided that his children were clumsy and irresponsible for breaking the lamp — attributing their behavior to their internal character traits rather than considering it was an accident.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly shows Steve making a dispositional (internal trait) attribution about his children's behavior.

sample_responses.response: Steve concludes that he is a mean person for disciplining his children, attributing his own behavior to his internal character.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — Steve makes a dispositional attribution about himself (internal characteristic of being mean).

sample_responses.response: Steve thought his children were probably just distracted by the TV when the lamp broke.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes a situational attribution (external distraction), which is the opposite of dispositional attribution.

parts.prompt: Explain how Ghrelin relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that for someone in the scenario, ghrelin levels are high when  they are hungry or low when they are not hungry.

required_elements: While the children waited for breakfast, ghrelin caused feelings of hunger.
alternatives.correct_example: While the children waited for breakfast, ghrelin caused feelings of hunger.

required_elements: Steve was able to lower his ghrelin levels because he ate all the breakfast.
alternatives.correct_example: Steve was able to lower his ghrelin levels because he ate all the breakfast.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must correctly apply the direction of ghrelin's relationship with hunger: ghrelin levels are HIGH when hungry and LOW when not hungry (or when eating reduces hunger). Responses that reverse this relationship (e.g., 'ghrelin levels were lower because they were hungry') do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: While the children waited for Steve to finish making breakfast, their ghrelin levels were high, causing feelings of hunger.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly states high ghrelin = hunger, applied to the children waiting for their meal.

sample_responses.response: After Steve ate all the breakfast, his ghrelin levels dropped because he was no longer hungry.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — correctly shows that eating reduces ghrelin (low ghrelin when not hungry).

sample_responses.response: The children's ghrelin levels were lower because they were hungry.
sample_responses.commentary: Reverses the relationship. Hunger is associated with HIGH ghrelin, not low ghrelin.

parts.prompt: Instead of asking the children what happened, Steve immediately turns off the television and sends them all outside to rake the large amount of leaves in the backyard. Steve stays irritable for the rest of the day. Explain how Authoritarian parenting style relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Steve is demonstrating a parenting style that includes  high control, no discussion of expectations or consequences (one-way  communication), or that focuses on obedience and discipline.

required_elements: Steve's parenting style most closely fits authoritarian because he was quick to punish them for the mistake they made (breaking the lamp) by making them stop playing to rake leaves. Steve was more interested in them following rules than understanding what happened.
alternatives.correct_example: Steve's parenting style most closely fits authoritarian because he was quick to punish them for the mistake they made (breaking the lamp) by making them stop playing to rake leaves. Steve was more interested in them following rules than understanding what happened.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must identify features of authoritarian parenting: high control, one-way communication (no discussion), emphasis on obedience and discipline. Steve punishing without explanation and not seeking to understand the situation exemplifies this style. Responses that say Steve explained his reasoning do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Steve's behavior is authoritarian because he immediately punished the children (raking leaves) without asking questions or discussing what happened — prioritizing obedience over understanding.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the key features of authoritarian parenting: punishment without explanation and one-way communication.

sample_responses.response: Steve was more interested in them following rules than understanding what happened — this high-control, no-discussion approach is characteristic of authoritarian parenting.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — explicitly contrasts rule enforcement with understanding, which captures the authoritarian style.

sample_responses.response: Steve explained to the children why he punished them.
sample_responses.commentary: Authoritarian parenting does not involve explaining or discussing consequences. This describes authoritative (warm but firm) parenting.

parts.prompt: Instead of asking the children what happened, Steve immediately turns off the television and sends them all outside to rake the large amount of leaves in the backyard. Steve stays irritable for the rest of the day. Explain how Steve's high level of the Big Five trait of neuroticism relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that an enduring characteristic of neuroticism (e.g., depression, negative feelings, anxiety) explains Steve's behavior in the scenario.

required_elements: Steve remains irritable all day and doesn't seem to recover from this minor event, indicating a lack of stability and high level of neuroticism. Steve experiences an extreme, negative emotional change throughout the scenario, showing that he is high in neuroticism. Steve gets overly upset at the sound of the lamp breaking.
alternatives.correct_example: Steve remains irritable all day and doesn't seem to recover from this minor event, indicating a lack of stability and high level of neuroticism. Steve experiences an extreme, negative emotional change throughout the scenario, showing that he is high in neuroticism. Steve gets overly upset at the sound of the lamp breaking.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must link neuroticism as an enduring personality trait (tendency toward negative emotions, emotional instability) to Steve's behavior. Simply saying Steve 'stayed irritable' without labeling it as a personality trait does not earn the point. Responses that misdefine neuroticism (e.g., 'neurotic about his kids' safety') or that describe a momentary reaction without connecting it to an enduring trait do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Steve's high neuroticism means he has an enduring tendency toward negative emotions. He gets overly upset at the crash and remains irritable all day, unable to recover from this minor event — consistent with the emotional instability characteristic of high neuroticism.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly attributes the persistent irritability to neuroticism as an enduring trait, explaining Steve's disproportionate and prolonged negative reaction.

sample_responses.response: Steve experiences an extreme negative emotional change and stays irritable all day, showing high neuroticism.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — links the enduring pattern (all-day irritability from a minor event) to the trait of neuroticism.

sample_responses.response: There is evidence that Steve is high in neuroticism because he stays irritable the rest of the day.
sample_responses.commentary: Simply restates observable behavior without explaining what neuroticism is or how it accounts for the behavior as an enduring trait.

sample_responses.response: Steve is neurotic about his kids and their safety.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses 'neurotic' in the colloquial sense (obsessively worried) rather than the Big Five sense (emotional instability and negative affect).
